explicit_text: My name is Shintaro Matsumoto, though my American name is Frank. I was born in Hiroshima Prefecture in 1899 and came to America when I was 13 years and 9 months old to join my father. I grew up in a small farm household in Japan, raised mostly by my grandmother and later my aunt. After arriving in America, I worked in various places and eventually started my own poultry business.
implicit_text: My name is Frank, though I was born in 1899 in a rural area of western Japan. I came to the United States when I was nearly 14 to join my father who had settled here. Growing up, I lived on a small farm and was mostly raised by my grandmother and later my aunt. After arriving in the U.S., I worked various jobs before eventually starting my own poultry business.

explicit_text: I was born in Hiroshima Prefecture in a small farm household. My early childhood was spent there, raised by my grandmother until she passed away. It was a rural area, and I attended school there until Emperor Meiji died in 1912, the same year I got my passport to come to America.

explicit_text: I came to America when I was almost 14 years old, summoned by my father who had been living here for some time. I arrived in San Francisco after sailing from Kobe and spent my early years in places like Sebastopol, California, working in apple drying and later poultry. America was a land of opportunity that many Japanese, including myself, were attracted to, even though life was sometimes hard and I had to become independent at a young age.

explicit_text: My father first went to Hawaii when I was about 2 years old before moving on to America. The ship I took to America stopped overnight in Honolulu, where we went to a Japanese bath house and restaurant. Many picture brides on the ship were also heading to Hawaii or America, showing how it was a key point of migration for Japanese people at the time.

explicit_text: I boarded the ship to America from Kobe, Japan, with my aunt accompanying me. Kobe was the port where my journey to America began in 1913, and I remember feeling very happy and hopeful about meeting my father and seeing America for the first time.

explicit_text: When I arrived in America, we landed in San Francisco after passing through Angel Island. I stayed in a hotel there for about three days, shopping and sightseeing before heading to Sebastopol. explicit_text: My father had a picture bride marriage, and I believe the bride came to America around Los Angeles. Although my father had never met her before, the marriage was arranged through pictures with my aunt acting as a go-between. This was part of the picture marriage custom common among Japanese immigrants at the time.

explicit_text: I traveled to America aboard a large ship called the Mongolia-Go. The voyage was challenging because the meals were Chinese food every day, which I was not used to and found hard to eat. I was one of the few young passengers on board, and many picture brides were also traveling on the ship, some heading to Hawaii and others to America.

explicit_text: When we arrived in San Francisco, we went through Angel Island for immigration and health inspection. Both my stepmother and I were found to have hookworms and were hospitalized there for a week until we passed the health test. It was also on Angel Island that I met my father for the first time, although I was not very excited because I had never seen him before.

explicit_text: After arriving in San Francisco, we went to Sebastopol where my father was running a small apple drying business with a few employees. Life there was rural and lonely, surrounded by apple orchards and cabins rather than nice houses. I started school there shortly after arriving, attending only during the winter while helping with the apple business in summer.

explicit_text: I started my poultry business around 1932 or 1933, but the Great Depression hit soon after. The economic downturn was severe and caused my business to go bankrupt within three to four years. It was a difficult time that affected many, including myself, as I tried to establish a livelihood in America.
